questions.question: Which ONE of the following pairings of ST elevation leads and ischemic territory is INCORRECT?
choices: ST elevation in II, III, aVF suggests inferior wall ischemia
ST elevation in V1–V4 suggests anterior septal ischemia
ST elevation in I, aVL, V5, V6 suggests lateral wall ischemia
Horizontal ST depression in V1–V3 may suggest posterior wall infarction
ST elevation in aVR alone is a finding specific to right ventricular infarction
questions.explanation: ST elevation in aVR may suggest left main coronary artery disease, proximal LAD disease, multivessel disease, or diffuse subendocardial ischemia, but it is not specific to right ventricular infarction. When RV infarction is suspected, V4R should be recorded in addition to inferior wall infarction findings.

questions.question: Select all correct pairings of ST elevation leads and culprit coronary arteries.
choices: ST elevation in V1–V4: suspect left anterior descending artery (LAD) disease
ST elevation in II, III, aVF: suspect right coronary artery (RCA) or left circumflex artery (LCX) disease
ST elevation in I, aVL, V5, V6: suspect LCX or diagonal branch disease
ST elevation in V1–V4: most typically suspect proximal RCA occlusion
ST elevation in V4R is useful for evaluating right ventricular infarction
questions.explanation: V1–V4 (anterior septum) → LAD; II, III, aVF (inferior wall) → RCA or LCX; I, aVL, V5, V6 (lateral wall) → LCX or diagonal branch. V4R is useful for evaluating RV infarction.

questions.question: Select all correct statements about inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI).
choices: ST elevation is seen in II, III, and aVF
The culprit vessel is most often the right coronary artery (RCA) or left circumflex artery (LCX)
Greater ST elevation in lead III than in lead II is more suggestive of RCA disease
When RV infarction is suspected, record right-sided precordial leads, particularly V4R
LAD proximal occlusion is the most typical cause of inferior ST elevation
questions.explanation: Inferior STEMI shows ST elevation in II, III, and aVF, with RCA or LCX most commonly responsible. Greater ST elevation in lead III than II suggests RCA disease; when RV infarction is suspected, add V4R. LAD proximal occlusion typically causes extensive anterior STEMI, not inferior.

questions.question: Which ONE of the following pairings of AHA coronary artery segment number and ECG findings is INCORRECT?
choices: #1 is the proximal RCA; occlusion can cause inferior wall infarction and RV infarction
#6 is the proximal LAD; occlusion can cause extensive anterior ST elevation
#11 is the proximal LCX; it can be involved in lateral, posterior, and inferior wall infarction
#5 is the left main coronary artery; occlusion or severe stenosis can cause aVR elevation and diffuse ST depression
#7 is the distal RCA and typically causes isolated ST elevation in V4R
questions.explanation: #7 is the mid LAD (left anterior descending artery), not the distal RCA. Mid LAD occlusion can cause anterior ST elevation. V4R ST elevation suggests RV infarction and is particularly important with proximal RCA occlusion.

questions.question: Which ONE of the following statements about the CHA2DS2-VASc score for stroke risk assessment in atrial fibrillation is INCORRECT?
choices: Heart failure: 1 point
Hypertension: 1 point
Age ≥75 years: 2 points
Prior stroke, TIA, or systemic thromboembolism: 2 points
Low serum potassium level: 2 points
questions.explanation: The CHA2DS2-VASc score evaluates heart failure, hypertension, age ≥75, diabetes, prior stroke/TIA/thromboembolism, vascular disease, age 65–74, and sex. Serum potassium level is not a component. Age ≥75 and prior stroke/TIA/thromboembolism are each worth 2 points.

questions.question: Select the 3 correct components of the CHA2DS2-VASc score.
choices: Diabetes mellitus
Vascular disease
Age 65–74 years
Hypokalemia
Elevated CRP
questions.explanation: In the CHA2DS2-VASc score, diabetes mellitus, vascular disease, and age 65–74 are each worth 1 point. Hypokalemia and elevated CRP may be clinically relevant as precipitants or background conditions in atrial fibrillation but are not components of the CHA2DS2-VASc score.

questions.question: Select all correct statements about ED management of atrial fibrillation.
choices: First assess blood pressure, level of consciousness, shock, ischemic chest discomfort, and acute heart failure
If hemodynamically unstable due to atrial fibrillation, consider synchronized cardioversion
For stable rapid ventricular response AF, consider rate control with beta-blockers, diltiazem, or verapamil depending on the clinical situation
Confirm time of onset, anticoagulation status, stroke risk, and bleeding risk
Even in unstable rapid ventricular response AF, wait several days for oral medications to take effect before considering cardioversion
questions.explanation: For rapid ventricular response AF, first assess hemodynamic stability. If hypotension, shock, ischemia, acute heart failure, or altered mental status is present and attributed to rapid AF, prioritize synchronized cardioversion. In stable cases, pursue rate control, identify underlying causes, and assess anticoagulation indications.

questions.question: Select all correct statements about acute management of atrial fibrillation, including rate control drug selection.
choices: Beta-blockers can be considered for stable rapid ventricular response AF with preserved left ventricular systolic function
Diltiazem or verapamil can be considered for stable rapid ventricular response AF with preserved LV systolic function
In heart failure with reduced LV systolic function, diltiazem or verapamil may worsen heart failure
For WPW syndrome with atrial fibrillation, verapamil should be given as the first-line rapid IV push
If hypotension or acute heart failure is present, consider electrical cardioversion in addition to pharmacological rate control
questions.explanation: For stable rapid ventricular response AF, beta-blockers, diltiazem, or verapamil are options depending on LV function and BP. Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers should be avoided in heart failure with reduced LV systolic function. If hypotension, acute heart failure, or other unstable findings are present, electrical therapy such as synchronized cardioversion should be considered in addition to pharmacological management. Verapamil and other AV node blocking agents are dangerous in WPW syndrome with AF and should not be used as first-line therapy.

questions.question: Which ONE of the following statements about ATP administration for stable paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT) in Japan is INCORRECT?
choices: For regular narrow-complex tachycardia with suspected PSVT and preserved blood pressure, first attempt vagal maneuvers
In Japan, adenosine triphosphate disodium hydrate (ATP-2Na) is commonly used rather than adenosine itself

In adults, with ECG monitoring and a three-way stopcock placed proximally, ATP-2Na 10 mg undiluted is rapidly injected IV followed immediately by a rapid 20 mL normal saline flush — this method is used in clinical practice
If 10 mg is ineffective, consider increasing to 20 mg while monitoring hemodynamics and ECG
Slow infusion of ATP maximizes the PSVT-terminating effect, so a saline flush is unnecessary
questions.explanation: For PSVT termination, ATP-2Na has an extremely short half-life and must be rapidly injected through a peripheral vein and immediately flushed with normal saline to reach the heart quickly. In adults, 10 mg is injected rapidly and flushed with 20 mL saline; if ineffective, 20 mg is considered. ECG monitoring, defibrillator, and emergency response equipment should be prepared. Caution is needed for asthma, severe bradycardia, AV block, and WPW with AF. Note that the package insert's general dosing method describes slow IV injection, which differs from the context of rapid administration for PSVT termination.

questions.question: Select all correct statements about the use of verapamil for PSVT.
choices: Verapamil suppresses AV nodal conduction and may be effective for terminating some types of PSVT
Hypotension may occur, so check blood pressure and hemodynamics before and after administration
In patients with suspected heart failure or reduced LV systolic function, verapamil may worsen the condition due to its negative inotropic effect
Avoid verapamil when wide-complex tachycardia or WPW syndrome with atrial fibrillation is suspected
Verapamil can always be safely given as a rapid IV bolus in infants, children, elderly patients, those with heart failure, and WPW patients
questions.explanation: Verapamil can be effective for PSVT, but requires attention to hypotension, bradycardia, AV block, and worsening heart failure. It is dangerous in wide-complex tachycardia, tachycardia where VT cannot be excluded, WPW with AF, reduced LV systolic function, and acute heart failure. In children, especially infants, there is a risk of severe circulatory depression and verapamil should be used cautiously.

questions.question: 胸痛患者の12誘導心電図でV1〜V3に水平型ST低下を認め、同誘導でR波が相対的に高く、T波が陽性であった。標準12誘導で明らかなST上昇はない。この所見から最も疑うべき病態はどれか。
choices: 前壁ST上昇型心筋梗塞
後壁心筋梗塞
急性心膜炎
Brugada症候群
questions.explanation: V1〜V3の水平型ST低下、高いR波、陽性T波は後壁梗塞の鏡像変化を示唆する。標準12誘導でST上昇が目立たなくても、後壁誘導V7〜V9を追加し、ST上昇を確認する。後壁梗塞は左回旋枝閉塞などでみられ、見逃すと再灌流療法の遅れにつながる。
